1: 1 Chronicles 10 is almost verbatim from 1 Samuel 31. But the Chronicler’s use of it is radically selective: everything from 1 Samuel 1-30 (Samuel’s career, Saul’s rise, his conflicts with David, the Witch of Endor, David’s Philistine alliance) is omitted. The Chronicler begins Israel’s royal narrative at the moment of Saul’s catastrophic defeat, treating it as the definitive statement of his reign before immediately transitioning to David. The battle of Mount Gilboa (in the Jezreel Valley) was one of the worst military defeats in Israel’s history — the Philistines effectively controlled the central highlands after it.
2: Jonathan, Abinadab, and Malchishua — three of Saul’s sons killed. The fourth son, Eshbaal (Ishbosheth), survived and would briefly rule north Israel under Abner’s regency (2 Sam 2-4). The Chronicler omits Ishbosheth entirely, keeping his focus on the Davidic transition. Jonathan’s death is particularly poignant given his covenant friendship with David (1 Samuel 18:3; 1 Chronicles 20:42), though the Chronicler does not pause on this.
3: The archers (môrîm baqqešet) hit Saul and wounded him. In 1 Samuel 31:3, the Hebrew is ambiguous between ‘archers’ and ‘shooters-with-the-bow’; Chronicles uses the same phrasing. Saul’s death-wound came from the anonymous Philistine archery — no individual hero kills Saul. The cowardly claim in 2 Samuel 1:1-16 (the Amalekite who claimed credit) is omitted by Chronicles, keeping the narrative clean.
4: Saul’s request to his armor-bearer: šᵉlōp̄ ḥarbekā ûdāqrēnî ḇāh — ‘draw your sword and run me through.’ Saul feared the uncircumcised Philistines would mock him (wᵉhitʿallᵉlû-ḇî — ‘abuse/mock/triumph over me’). His armor-bearer refused for fear. Saul fell on his own sword — suicide by falling on one’s own weapon was the honorable death for a defeated king in the ANE, avoiding capture and degradation. The armor-bearer then did the same.
5: The armor-bearer dies alongside his master. The loyalty of the armor-bearer — choosing death over abandonment of the king — is a minor act of faithfulness that contrasts with the faithlessness the Chronicler will attribute to Saul (1 Chronicles 10:13). Even in death, Saul’s servant was more loyal than Saul was to YHWH.
6: wayyāmot šāʾûl ûšᵉlōšet bānāyw ûkol-bêtô yaḥdāw mētû — ‘So Saul and his three sons died, and all his house died together.’ The Chronicler expands 1 Samuel 31:6 (‘and all his men’) to ‘all his house.’ This is the theological climax of Saul’s narrative: the extinction of the Saulide ruling house. YHWH’s judgment is total. In Chronicles, this verse marks the complete end of the Saulide dynasty — there is no space for Ishbosheth’s brief counter-reign or David’s loyalty to Jonathan’s son.
7: The men of Israel in the valley and beyond the Jordan fled; the Philistines occupied the abandoned cities. This geographical note — valley + trans-Jordan — shows the scope of the military collapse. The Philistine occupation of Israelite cities was not just military defeat but territorial seizure, reverting the land to enemy hands. The phrase wayyāḇōʾû pᵉlištîm wayyēšᵉḇû bāhem (‘the Philistines came and dwelt in them’) is the nadir — a reversal of the conquest.
8: The next day the Philistines found Saul’s fallen body. wayᵉpaššᵉṭû ʾet-haḥălālîm — ‘they stripped the slain.’ Stripping the dead was standard ANE battle practice — collecting armor, weapons, and valuables from fallen enemies. Finding the king’s body was a major trophy. The Chronicler follows 1 Samuel 31:8-10 closely here.
9: They stripped Saul and took his head and armor, sending messengers throughout Philistia to carry the news to their idols and their people. lᵉḇaśśēr ʾet-ʿăṣabbêhem wᵉʾet-hāʿām — ‘to bring good news to their idols and to the people.’ The word ʿăṣabbîm (idols, literally ‘labored things/grief-things’) is a contemptuous term. Philistine victory was credited to their gods — especially Dagon. The news circuit paralleled Israel’s own practice of celebrating YHWH’s victories.
10: Saul’s armor was deposited in the house of their gods, and his head was fastened in the temple of Dagon. The trophy display of a defeated king in an enemy temple was an ANE practice of theological humiliation — demonstrating that the enemy’s god had overcome the defeated nation’s deity. The irony is sharp: YHWH had already demonstrated his superiority over Dagon when the ark was placed in Dagon’s temple (1 Samuel 5:1-5), and yet here YHWH’s king’s head adorns Dagon’s temple. The Chronicler’s 1 Chronicles 10:13-14 will address this apparent theological reversal.
11: When the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead heard what the Philistines had done to Saul — all the valiant men arose and took Saul’s body and his sons’ bodies and brought them to Jabesh. Jabesh-gilead had a special covenant relationship with Saul: he had rescued them in his first act as king (1 Sam 11) from Nahash the Ammonite. Their loyalty here — at great personal risk — fulfills the covenant debt. Even after Saul’s death, his first act of royal faithfulness elicited this final act of human loyalty.
12: The valiant men of Jabesh brought the bodies, buried the bones under the oak in Jabesh, and fasted seven days. The seven-day mourning fast (wayyāṣûmû šiḇʿat yāmîm) was the full period of mourning (Genesis 50:10). The burial ‘under the oak’ in Jabesh was an honorable interment — the oak was a sacred tree associated with covenant (Genesis 35:8; Joshua 24:26). Chronicles omits the later detail of David recovering the bones for final burial at Zela (2 Samuel 21:12-14).
13: wayyāmot šāʾûl bᵉmaʿlô ʾăšer māʿal baYHWH — ‘So Saul died for his unfaithfulness (maʿal) which he committed against the LORD.’ This verse and 1 Chronicles 10:14 are the Chronicler’s unique addition, absent from 1 Sam 31. Three specific offenses: (1) māʿal baYHWH — general unfaithfulness; (2) not keeping the word of YHWH (referring to 1 Sam 15, Saul’s incomplete destruction of Amalek, which Chronicles has not narrated but assumes knowledge of); (3) lišʾôl bāʾôḇ lᵉḏrōš — consulting a medium to inquire. The medium (ʾôḇ) consultation (1 Sam 28) was explicitly forbidden (Leviticus 19:31; 1 Chronicles 20:6; Deuteronomy 18:11). Saul sought the dead instead of the Living God.
14: wᵉlōʾ ḏāraš baYHWH wayyᵉmîtēhû wayyasēb ʾet-hammalḵût lᵉḏāwîḏ ben-yišay — ‘and did not inquire of the LORD; therefore he killed him and turned the kingdom over to David son of Jesse.’ The verb dāraš (to seek, inquire of, resort to) is the Chronicler’s central theological diagnostic for good kingship. Kings who seek (dāraš) the LORD prosper; those who do not are destroyed. This verse sets up the entire Davidic narrative that follows: David’s characteristic quality will be exactly this seeking of the LORD. The kingdom transfer — wayyasēb ʾet-hammalḵût (he turned/transferred the kingdom) — is attributed directly to YHWH’s action, not David’s political maneuvering.
